one_line_takeaway: A ~$42 blackbox query campaign lets an attacker reverse-engineer an LLM watermark and then both forge and remove it at >80% success, breaking schemes (KGW2-SELFHASH) thought safe and demolishing the assumed spoofing-vs-scrubbing robustness tradeoff.
key_finding: For a one-time query cost under $50 (~$42 at Jan-2024 ChatGPT pricing, n=30,000 queries), the attacker both spoofs and scrubs state-of-the-art schemes previously considered safe, with average success >80%. KGW2-SELFHASH spoofing reaches >80% high-quality watermarked-detected text at FPR=1e-3. Scrubbing KGW2-SELFHASH in the hard long-text (>1000 token) regime jumps from ~0% (base DIPPER) to >80% (DIPPER+OURS), while NO baseline exceeds ~25-30%. Crucially, this refutes the previously assumed spoofing/scrubbing robustness TRADEOFF: any vulnerability to stealing enables BOTH attacks simultaneously, so the two are not at odds. Even harmful text can be spoofed: a weakly-aligned MISTRAL-7B attacker (4% refusal) imprints watermarks on harmful queries despite learning only from a 100%-refusal-rate aligned LMmo, enabling reputational/incrimination harm.
core_contribution: The paper identifies 'watermark stealing' (WS) as a fundamental vulnerability of distribution-modifying LLM watermarks and proposes the FIRST automated WS algorithm. By querying only the blackbox API of a watermarked LLM (no secret key, no detector needed), the attacker reverse-engineers an approximate model of the watermarking rules in the form of a scoring function s*(T, [T1T2T3]) that estimates whether a candidate token T is 'green' in a given context. The authors' own novel insight (distinct from cited prior work) is that this single stolen model boosts BOTH downstream attacks: spoofing (forging watermarked text) AND scrubbing (removing watermarks). Prior work discussed stealing-like ideas only for spoofing; the authors are first to show it also greatly boosts scrubbing, and first to demonstrate a practical attack on the KGW2-SELFHASH scheme previously believed safe.
threat_model: Two actors: a model owner running a proprietary instruction-tuned LLM (LMmo) watermarked with secret key xi, and an attacker with only BLACKBOX access to full generations of LMmo (realistic API model), aware a watermark exists. Following Kerckhoffs' principle, the attacker knows all scheme parameters but NOT xi, and has an auxiliary open model LMatt. Two threat-model dimensions: (1) Detector access -- D0 (no detector access, most restrictive) vs D1 (binary detector API, no z-score exposure); (2) Base-response availability -- B0 (only watermarked API) vs B1 (access to non-watermarked/pre-deployment corpus). Main experiments target the MOST restrictive and realistic (D0, B0) setting. Attacker goal is targeted: spoof text answering a chosen prompt, or scrub a given watermarked text, at very low false-positive rate (f <= 1e-3).
limitations: Authors' own caveats: (1) Some attack features are tuned specifically for KGW2-SELFHASH; KGW2-SUM is harder to spoof (only ~54% vs >80%), so results are scheme-dependent. (2) Scope is limited to distribution-modifying watermarks (KGW family, UNIGRAM); semantic, sampling-modification, and NN-based schemes are out of scope. (3) They do NOT consider a detector that returns the exact z-score (would directly expose green/red lists). (4) Boosting PEGASUS is notably less effective than DIPPER. (5) Text quality is judged by GPT4-as-judge and P-SP as proxies for human preference, not human evaluation. (6) No LLM watermarks are actually deployed yet, so the attack is validated on research schemes rather than a production system. Two concurrent works (Pang et al. 2024; Wu & Chandrasekaran 2024) study related settings the authors distinguish from theirs.

datasets_used: C4 RealNewsLike subset (Raffel et al. 2020) -- 30,000 prompts used for the stealing/query phase, disjoint from evaluation sets
Dolly-CW (writing prompts, 100 examples) -- spoofing and scrubbing
MMW BookReports and MMW FakeNews (from Mark My Words benchmark, Piet et al. 2023) -- spoofing and scrubbing
Reddit WritingPrompts (Verma et al. 2024, 100 examples) -- scrubbing (long texts)
quantitative_results: Spoofing KGW2-SELFHASH (B0,D0), MISTRAL-7B attacker: FPR*@1e-3 = 0.81 (Dolly-CW / LMmo=LLAMA-7B), avg >80%; GPT4 quality ~8.26, median p-value 2e-19
Spoofing KGW2-SUM (harder, MISTRAL-7B): FPR*@1e-3 = 0.54 (Dolly-CW / LLAMA-7B), still >50%
Spoofing other schemes (MISTRAL-7B, LLAMA-7B, Dolly-CW): UNIGRAM 0.79, KGW-HARD 0.89, KGW-SOFT 0.82, KGW2-SELFHASH 0.81
Scrubbing KGW2-SELFHASH long texts: DIPPER baseline FNR*@1e-3 ~0.00-0.03; DIPPER+OURS = 0.90 (Dolly-CW/LLAMA-7B), 0.76-0.90 across settings, median p-value >=0.03
Scrubbing baselines all weak: best (RECURSIVE DIPPER, CHATGPT) <=~0.30; none reach 25% avg claim
Scrubbing across schemes with DIPPER+OURS: UNIGRAM up to 0.94, KGW-SOFT up to 0.85, KGW2-SUM up to 0.98 (near 100%)
Query cost: n=30,000 queries = $42 (Jan-2024 ChatGPT pricing); success curves converge around 10,000 queries
Ablation (no partial-context scores, w1=w2=0): average spoofing success drops from ~80% to <50% at n=30,000
Spoofing-on-existing-text via DIPPER imprinting: >=74% success at FPR 1e-3 on KGW2-SELFHASH and KGW-SOFT
top-1-of-5 selection (D1 setting) can push spoofing to nearly 100%
baselines_compared: DIPPER paraphraser (Krishna et al. 2023) -- scrubbing baseline and boosted target
PEGASUS paraphraser (Zhang et al. 2019) -- scrubbing baseline and boosted target
Recursive DIPPER (5 rounds, Sadasivan et al. 2023) -- scrubbing baseline
Ablated attacker without partial-context scores (w1=w2=0) -- internal baseline
Watermark schemes attacked: KGW-HARD, KGW-SOFT, KGW2-SELFHASH (main target), KGW2-SUM, UNIGRAM
